did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a customized Inception V3 deep learning model (Convolutional Neural Network) with Bayesian hyperparameter tuning to classify skin lesions (Melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma) using the ISIC 2024 and HAM10000 datasets.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development of a customized Inception V3 deep learning model with Bayesian hyperparameter tuning. It details specific methods including data preprocessing, augmentation, and training on the ISIC 2024 and HAM10000 datasets, and reports original quantitative results (e.g., 95.1% accuracy) rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: An Efficient Skin Cancer Stage Diagnostic Approach Using Customized Inception V3 Deep Learning Model
journal: International Journal of Advanced Computer Science and Applications
brief_summary: This study developed a customized Inception V3 deep learning model with Bayesian hyperparameter tuning to classify Melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma using the ISIC 2024 and HAM10000 datasets. The approach integrated data augmentation to address class imbalance and reduce overfitting, alongside probabilistic optimization to enhance identification accuracy. The model was evaluated on combined datasets using metrics including accuracy, precision, sensitivity, specificity, and F1 score. The proposed method achieved a combined average accuracy of 95.1%, a precision of 94.42%, a sensitivity of 97.3%, a specificity of 98.8%, and an F1 score of 95.7%. These results demonstrate that the customized model significantly outperforms existing techniques and standard pre-trained models in diagnosing pigmented skin lesions.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the three main malignant skin tumors classified in the study. Included in ISIC 2024 and HAM10000 datasets. The paper notes it is considered the worst among the three types with a bad prognosis.
One of the three main malignant skin tumors classified in the study. Included in ISIC 2024 and HAM10000 datasets. The dataset description mentions 1,500 Basal Cell Carcinomas in the collection used.

One of the three main malignant skin tumors classified in the study. Included in ISIC 2024 and HAM10000 datasets. The dataset description mentions 2,000 squamous cell skin lesions in the collection used.

Mentioned in the context of dataset characteristics: 'Notably, the collection is unbalanced, with around half of the images displaying melanocytic nevus.' However, the study explicitly states its objective is to classify Melanoma, BCC, and SCC, and Table IV lists only these three classes for the case studies.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories of skin cancer (Melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the objective is to 'classify skin lesions and differentiate between malignant Melanoma, BCC, and SCC.' The model uses a Softmax layer to output probabilities for these three discrete classes. It is a multiclass classification task evaluated using accuracy, precision, sensitivity, specificity, and F1 score.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2024
datasets_used.description: A publicly available collection of dermoscopic and medical images managed by the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC), released in 2024. The study utilized a subset of this dataset containing dermoscopic images resized to 299x299x3 pixels.

use.training: Used for training the customized Inception V3 model with data augmentation and Bayesian hyperparameter tuning.
use.validation: Used for validation during the training process (combined with testing split as reported in Table IV).
use.testing: Used for testing the model performance (combined with validation split as reported in Table IV).
use.other: Combined with HAM10000 dataset for the final evaluation.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A large collection of multi-source dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions, managed by the International Skin Imaging Collaboration. The study utilized a subset of this dataset.

use.training: Used for training the customized Inception V3 model with data augmentation and Bayesian hyperparameter tuning.
use.validation: Used for validation during the training process (combined with testing split as reported in Table IV).
use.testing: Used for testing the model performance (combined with validation split as reported in Table IV).
use.other: Combined with ISIC 2024 dataset for the final evaluation.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics (accuracy, precision, sensitivity, specificity, F1 score) and qualitative comparisons with existing techniques.

Customized Inception V3.accuracy: Inconsistent; lacked accuracy while identifying two classes; confused one class for another; prone to overfitting

Case I (Without Augmentation).notes: Table III details the dataset split for Case I: Melanoma (1300 total, 910 training, 390 testing), BCC (1500 total, 1050 training, 450 testing), SCC (2000 total, 1400 training, 600 testing). Figure 4 shows accuracy curves.

Case II (With Augmentation).notes: Data augmentation stabilized validation accuracy and diminished overfitting. Figure 5 shows accuracy curves. Table IV details the dataset split.

Case III (With Augmentation and Bayesian.notes: Bayesian tuning enhanced decision-making and stability. Figure 6 shows accuracy curves. Table V details per-class metrics.

Overall Combined Results (Case III).notes: The model significantly outperformed existing techniques and standard pre-trained models. Results indicate more accurate and consistent diagnosis of pigmented skin lesions compared to current standards.

conclusions: The proposed customized Inception V3 model with Bayesian hyperparameter tuning and data augmentation significantly outperformed existing techniques and standard pre-trained models in diagnosing pigmented skin lesions.
The model achieved a combined average accuracy of 95.1%, precision of 94.42%, sensitivity of 97.3%, specificity of 98.8%, and F1 score of 95.7% on the combined ISIC 2024 and HAM10000 datasets.
Data augmentation effectively addressed class disparity and reduced overfitting, leading to more stable validation accuracy and diminished disparity between training and validation accuracy.
Bayesian hyperparameter tuning enhanced the model's decision-making by incorporating prediction confidence, resulting in smoother accuracy predictions and reduced volatility.
The approach provides a more accurate and consistent diagnosis of Melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma compared to current standards and previous research efforts.

The study demonstrates that integrating data augmentation, transfer learning, and probabilistic optimization into a single end-to-end framework improves resilience and generalization for multiclass skin cancer classification.
important_remarks: The study combines the ISIC 2024 and HAM10000 datasets but does not explicitly detail the protocol for preventing data leakage between the two sources during the split, raising potential overlap concerns.

The HAM10000 dataset is noted as unbalanced, with approximately half of the images displaying melanocytic nevus, yet the model was trained only on Melanoma, BCC, and SCC, potentially ignoring a significant portion of the source data's distribution.
The paper lacks formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or confidence intervals) to validate the significance of the performance improvements over baseline models.

The dataset split for Case II (1625 Melanoma, 1875 BCC, 2500 SCC) differs from the raw counts mentioned in the dataset description (1300 Melanoma, 1500 BCC, 2000 SCC), suggesting data augmentation or subset selection occurred before the final split, which may affect generalizability.

The model was evaluated exclusively on public dermoscopic datasets (ISIC 2024 and HAM10000), with no external validation on independent clinical cohorts, limiting evidence of real-world clinical applicability.

The paper claims the model 'significantly outperformed' existing techniques but relies on qualitative comparisons and average metrics rather than rigorous statistical benchmarks against specific state-of-the-art baselines on the same test sets.
